g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture covers polynomial representation using linked lists, a crucial data structures topic.
A polynomial is a mathematical expression with terms containing coefficients and exponents, like 5x³ + 4x² + 2x + 1.
g.text: A polynomial is a mathematical expression with terms, each having:

g.data-narration: Arrays waste massive memory for sparse polynomials. For 3x¹⁰⁰⁰ + 2x + 7, an array needs 1001 slots with 998 zeros!
Linked lists store only non-zero terms! For 3x¹⁰⁰⁰ + 2x + 7, we need just 3 nodes instead of 1001 array slots.
g.text: Solution: Linked List Stores Only Non-Zero Terms

g.data-narration: Compare array and linked list: arrays need O(d) memory regardless of terms, while linked lists use O(n) for non-zero terms only.
Each polynomial node contains three fields: coefficient (coeff), exponent (expo), and a pointer to the next term (next).
Terms are stored in decreasing exponent order (highest power first). This enables efficient polynomial operations.
g.text: Important: Terms Stored in Decreasing Exponent Order

g.data-narration: The insertTerm function maintains sorted order, handles same exponents by adding coefficients, and removes terms with zero result.
g.text: If list empty OR new expo > first expo
If expo equals existing → add coeffs

g.data-narration: Polynomial addition is like merging two sorted lists. Compare exponents, take larger, or add if equal. Skip zero results.
g.text: Similar to merge sort's merge step - both polynomials sorted in decreasing order:

g.data-narration: Multiplication: Each term of P1 multiplies with every term of P2. New coeff = product, new expo = sum. insertTerm combines like terms.
g.text: For each term in P1, multiply with all terms in P2:
newCoeff = coeff1 × coeff2 | newExpo = expo1 + expo2

g.data-narration: To evaluate P(x), sum coeff × x^expo for all terms. For P(2)=5(2)³+4(2)²+2(2)+1, the result is 61.
g.text: for all terms in the polynomial

g.data-narration: Remember these complexities: Addition O(n1+n2), Multiplication O(n1×n2), Evaluation O(n×k).
Key takeaways: Use linked lists for sparse polynomials, store terms in decreasing exponent order, skip zero coefficients after addition.

g.text: • Node structure: coeff, expo, next pointer
• Terms stored in decreasing exponent order
• Linked list: O(n) memory vs O(d) for arrays
• Addition: compare exponents, merge like merge-sort
• Always skip zero coefficients after operations
Free memory using freePolynomial() to prevent memory leaks!

g.data-narration: Always free dynamically allocated memory to prevent leaks. Traverse and free each node individually.
g.text: Memory Management: Always Free Allocated Memory
